title: Anisotropic Crystallization Kinetics and Interfacial Dynamics of Phase-Change Material Sb$_2$S$_3$ from Machine Learning Force Field Simulations
authors: Souvik Chakraborty, Wen-Qing Li, Yun Liu
abstract: The phase-change material antimony sulfide (Sb$_2$S$_3$) relies on rapid and reversible phase transitions between crystalline and amorphous states, which are critical for their performance in data storage and photonics applications. In this work, a machine learning force field is developed based on the moment tensor potential approach, allowing us to understand the atomistic origin of the structural evolution and crystallization kinetics in Sb$_2$S$_3$ for the first time, by enabling large-scale molecular dynamics simulations (up to 7680 atoms for 40 ns). Sb$_2$S$_3$ shows anisotropic growth rates with the [100] facet exhibiting the fastest growth due to the strong Sb-S covalent bonding along its quasi-1D ribbon-like structure of its crystalline phase. The activation energy for crystal growth is found to be 0.55-0.57 eV, whereas that for diffusion is around 1.16-1.56 eV. The lower activation energy for crystal growth indicates that its heterogeneous crystallization is interface controlled rather than diffusion limited, unlike GST and GeTe with atomic attachment at the solid-liquid interface being energetically favoured over long range atomic transport. These findings provide key insights into the structural, thermodynamic, and kinetic properties of Sb$_2$S$_3$, paving the way for optimizing its functionality including switching speed, reliability, and energy efficiency.
summary: This paper uses machine learning force fields to perform large-scale simulations of the phase-change material Sb2S3. It reveals that the material's crystallization is governed by interface-controlled kinetics and highly anisotropic growth. These insights are crucial for improving the speed and efficiency of next-generation data storage and neuromorphic computing devices.
main_problem: Understanding the atomistic-scale mechanisms, structural evolution, and anisotropic crystallization kinetics of the phase-change material Sb2S3 during the crystal-to-liquid phase transition.
main_result: The study finds that Sb2S3 crystallization is interface-controlled rather than diffusion-limited, with the [100] facet exhibiting the fastest growth due to strong covalent bonding along its 1D ribbon structure.
method: Large-scale molecular dynamics simulations using a Machine Learning Force Field (MLFF) developed via the Moment Tensor Potential (MTP) approach and active learning.
model_or_system: The physical system is the phase-change material antimony sulfide (Sb2S3), which transitions between a crystalline (Pnma space group) and an amorphous/liquid phase. The simulation uses a Dual Phase Coexistence (DPC) model with up to 7680 atoms.
key_observables: Lattice parameters, radial distribution functions (RDF), angular distribution functions (ADF), coordination numbers, crystal growth rates (Vg), and activation energies for growth and diffusion.
important_parameters: Activation energy for growth (0.55-0.57 eV), activation energy for diffusion (1.16-1.56 eV), melting point (975 K), and optimal undercooling (~100 K).
main_assumptions: The accuracy of the results depends on the quality of the MTP training set; the study assumes the MLFF-MD workflow effectively bridges microscopic dynamics to macroscopic growth.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows MTP training correlation, the Sb2S3 unit cell, and liquid phase snapshots/RDFs; Figure 2 displays angle and coordination number distributions for crystal and liquid phases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material and problem, describes the development and validation of the MTP machine learning force field, presents structural and kinetic results of the phase transition, and discusses the implications for device optimization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 3. a) Crystal growth simulation of the dual phase coexistence (DPC) model with 7680 atoms. The evolution of crystal growth (normal to [010] interface) is illustrated by the snapshots taken from simulation trajectory (700 K) at 0, 10, and 40 ns respectively. The final equilibrated box dimension is 46.04 Å × 90.74 Å × 47.10 Å. For other facets, the growth direction remained parallel to the respective elongated box vector. b) Snaps of the three types of facets of Sb2S3 crystal at crystal-liquid interfaces considered in this study. c) The growth rate as a function of temperature for three types of interfacial crystal facets [100], [010], and [001] respectively. Figure 4. a) Wilson-Frenkel fitting (Equation 2) of the growth rate data for all three facets and the facet average. The fitting curves are represented by the dashed lines. From the fitting, the activation energy barrier for growth (ΔEaWF) was computed. b) Free energy difference between crystal & liquid (Δµ) as a function of temperature from Thompson-Spaepen formula (Equation 3).
